title: Ojja with merguez
subtitle: Tunisian spiced tomato and egg with merguez sausages
excerpt: Merguez sausages cooked in a spiced tomato and pepper sauce, then eggs cracked in and cooked through in the sauce. A Tunisian version of shakshuka with the sausage integral to the dish rather than optional. Harissa in the base gives the sauce its heat and distinctive North African character.
sourceNotes: Ojja is the Tunisian egg and vegetable dish cooked in a spiced tomato sauce. The version with merguez is the most common — the sausage fat bastes the sauce as it cooks, giving it a distinctive richness. Ojja differs from the better-known shakshuka primarily in the use of harissa and, traditionally, the inclusion of ground lamb or merguez.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The tomato and sausage sauce can be made up to 2 days ahead. Add the eggs fresh when reheating.

content.text: Slice the merguez into rounds rather than leaving them whole — this gives more surface area for browning and distributes the sausage flavour through the sauce. Brown them well before adding the tomatoes: the rendered fat is the base flavour of the dish.

The eggs are added last, as in shakshuka. Cover the pan for the eggs: 4 to 5 minutes for runny yolks, 6 to 7 for set. Remove from the heat while the whites are just set and serve immediately — the sauce continues to cook the eggs from below.
Build the sauce and cook the eggs

Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a wide, deep pan over medium-high heat. Add the {{merguez}} rounds and cook for 5 minutes until well browned, stirring occasionally. Remove and set aside. In the same pan, add the {{onion}} and {{pepper-red}} and cook for 8 minutes until soft. Add the {{garlic}}, {{harissa}}, {{cumin-ground}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} and cook for 1 minute. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}} and 100 ml of water. Simmer for 10 minutes until the sauce thickens slightly. Return the merguez to the pan. Make 4 wells in the sauce and break an {{eggs}} into each. Cover and cook for 4 to 5 minutes until the whites are set. Scatter with {{parsley-flat}} and serve directly from the pan with bread.

items.cause: Left on the heat too long or not watching
items.fix: Check after 4 minutes. The whites should be opaque but the yolk should still move when the pan is tilted. The sauce continues cooking the eggs after the heat is off

items.cause: Tomatoes too watery or insufficient reduction time
items.fix: Simmer uncovered for an extra 5 minutes before adding the eggs. The sauce should be thick enough to hold the eggs without them sinking to the bottom

items.cause: Merguez already very spicy plus harissa
items.fix: Use only 0.5 tbsp of harissa with spicy merguez. Taste the sauce before adding eggs and adjust

content.text: Ojja is an everyday Tunisian dish, eaten for lunch or dinner with bread and salad. The merguez version is the most popular, though ojja bil kefta (with minced meat balls) and ojja bil gambari (with prawns) are also common. It has structural similarities to shakshuka (both are eggs cooked in a spiced tomato base) but the harissa and merguez give the Tunisian version a heat and richness that distinguishes it. In France, where Tunisian food has had significant influence, ojja appears in couscous restaurants and in the home kitchens of the diaspora alongside brik and lablabi.
